title: The Day of the Lord Will Come
subtitle: Scoffers in the last days, divine patience, and the new heavens and new earth

heb.paragraph: Scoffers will come in the last days - they ridicule the promise of Christ return. Mockery is their weapon against hope.
Following their own sinful desires - the scoffers are motivated by self-interest. Denial of judgment frees them for indulgence.
The heavens existed long ago and the earth was formed out of water - creation by divine word. The same word destroyed the world by flood.

panels.ctx: This is now the second letter Peter writes to stir up sincere minds by way of reminder - to remember the predictions of the holy prophets and the commandment of the Lord through the apostles. First of all, scoffers will come in the last days, following their own sinful desires, saying: Where is the promise of his coming? For ever since the fathers fell asleep, all things are continuing as they were from the beginning. They deliberately overlook that the heavens existed long ago and the earth was formed out of water and through water by the word of God, and that by these waters the world was deluged and perished. By the same word the present heavens and earth are stored up for fire, kept until the day of judgment and destruction of the ungodly.

cross.note: In the last time there will be scoffers, following their own ungodly passions.
Let him be quick, let him speed his work that we may see it - mockery of divine action.
The waters and the formation of earth - creation by word.
The flood narrative - destruction by water.

notes.note: This is Peter second letter to stir up sincere minds. Remember the prophets and the apostolic commandment.
Scoffers will come, following sinful desires: Where is the promise of his coming? All things continue as from the beginning.
They overlook creation and flood by divine word. The present heavens and earth are stored up for fire, kept for judgment day.
Calvin notes that the scoffers use apparent stability as evidence against divine intervention. They confuse patience with inaction.
The flood refutes uniformitarianism. God has intervened catastrophically before; he will again. Water once, fire next.
The fathers falling asleep likely refers to the first generation of Christians who expected Christ imminent return and have now died.
Fire as the instrument of final judgment appears also in Stoic thought (ekpyrosis) but Peter ground is OT and Jewish apocalyptic.
Schreiner emphasizes that Peter addresses the delay of the parousia - a pastoral problem in the early church. His answer is theological, not chronological.
Davids notes that the scoffers argument is essentially naturalistic: since the world keeps running normally, no supernatural intervention will occur.

sections.header: Verses 8-13 - "The Day of the Lord Will Come"

heb.paragraph: The Lord is patient toward you - divine delay is mercy, not weakness. God waits for repentance.
The day of the Lord will come like a thief - unexpected, sudden, inescapable. No advance warning for the unprepared.
New heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells - not mere renovation but renewal. The new order is characterized by justice.

panels.ctx: Do not overlook this: with the Lord one day is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day. The Lord is not slow concerning his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish but that all should reach repentance. But the day of the Lord will come like a thief - the heavens will pass away with a roar, the heavenly bodies will be burned up and dissolved, and the earth and the works done on it will be exposed. Since all these things are thus to be dissolved, what sort of people ought you to be in lives of holiness and godliness, waiting for and hastening the coming of the day of God? The heavens will be set on fire and dissolved, the heavenly bodies will melt. But according to his promise we are waiting for new heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells.

cross.note: A thousand years in your sight are but as yesterday when it is past.
The day of the Lord will come like a thief in the night.
Behold, I create new heavens and a new earth.
Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first had passed away.

notes.note: One day as a thousand years; a thousand years as one day. The Lord is patient, not wishing any to perish but all to reach repentance.
The day of the Lord will come like a thief. Heavens pass away with a roar, heavenly bodies burn and dissolve, earth exposed.
Since all will be dissolved, live in holiness, waiting for and hastening the day of God. We wait for new heavens and new earth where righteousness dwells.
Calvin emphasizes divine patience as the reason for delay. God desires salvation, not destruction. Time is grace.
The dissolution of the present order should produce holiness, not despair. Knowing the end should transform the present.
Not wishing any to perish expresses God redemptive intention. The delay is missionary opportunity.
Hastening the day is a striking phrase. Human repentance and mission may accelerate God timetable.
Schreiner emphasizes that vv. 8-9 are central to biblical eschatology. Divine patience explains the delay; the delay serves salvation.
Davids notes the ethical implication: cosmic dissolution relativizes earthly attachments. Holiness is the proper response to eschatology.

heb.paragraph: Be diligent to be found by him without spot or blemish - the opposite of the false teachers (2:13). Moral purity for the parousia.
The ignorant and unstable twist his letters - Paul writings can be misunderstood. False teachers exploit difficulty.
Grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord - growth is the antidote to apostasy. Stability comes through progress.

panels.ctx: Therefore, beloved, since you are waiting for these things, be diligent to be found by him without spot or blemish, and at peace. Count the patience of our Lord as salvation, just as our beloved brother Paul also wrote to you according to the wisdom given him, as he does in all his letters, speaking of these things. There are some things in them hard to understand, which the ignorant and unstable twist to their own destruction, as they do the other Scriptures. You therefore, beloved, knowing this beforehand, take care that you are not carried away with the error of lawless people and lose your own stability. But grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. To him be the glory both now and to the day of eternity. Amen.

cross.note: Do you presume on the riches of his kindness and patience?
So that he may establish your hearts blameless in holiness at the coming of our Lord.
Walk in a manner worthy of the Lord, bearing fruit and increasing in the knowledge of God.
Do your best to present yourself to God as one approved, rightly handling the word of truth.

notes.note: Be diligent to be found spotless and at peace. Count patience as salvation. Paul also wrote this - some things are hard to understand, which the ignorant twist.
Knowing this beforehand, take care not to be carried away by error and lose stability. Grow in grace and knowledge. To him be glory forever.
Calvin notes that difficulty in Paul is acknowledged. This does not justify abandoning Scripture but demands careful interpretation.
Growth is the safeguard against error. Stability is not static but dynamic - maintained by continual advance in grace.
Our beloved brother Paul - Peter acknowledges Paul authority and affirms their common message despite the Galatians 2 confrontation.
The other Scriptures - Paul letters are implicitly granted scriptural status, an early witness to canonical recognition.
Schreiner emphasizes the canonical significance of v. 16. Peter treats Paul letters as Scripture - a remarkable statement within the NT itself.
Davids notes that the letter ends not with polemic but with positive exhortation: grow in grace. The goal is maturity, not merely avoidance of error.

rows.label: vv. 1-7
rows.text: Scoffers; creation and flood; fire reserved

rows.label: vv. 8-13
rows.text: Divine patience; day of the Lord; new heavens and earth

rows.label: vv. 14-18
rows.text: Be spotless; Paul witness; grow in grace

lit.note: Chapter 3 answers the scoffers with theology of divine patience and certain judgment. The letter closes with a call to growth.

themes.note: Judgment and sovereignty dominate: God controls time and will act decisively. Mercy appears in divine patience. Prophecy is strong in the eschatological vision.
